text: A total of seven ports were utilized, four abdominal ports followed by a further three thoracic ports.
Upon creation of pneumoperitoneum and entry into the abdomen, a quick diagnostic laparoscopy was performed which revealed a diaphragmatic rent
with herniation of stomach and omentum.
With the help of a non-traumatic grasper, attempts were made to reduce the contents but tense adhesions were encountered.
We then proceeded to widen the diaphragmatic rent further using first a monopolar energy source followed by an ultrasonic scalpel.
On opening up the rent, seropurulent fluid was found pouring from the thoracic cavity into the abdomen.
The widening of the defect enabled the reduction of the herniated omentum and we were then able to pass our scope through the rent and into the thorax.
The entire left pleural cavity was filled with stomach contents, pus and infected fluid which was promptly aspirated.
This was followed by further release of adhesions and attempts to reduce the herniated stomach which were unsuccessful.
The decision was then made to opt for a thoracic approach and a thoracic optical port was inserted which further revealed the extent of local contamination.
The entire left lung, the heart and the left thoracic cavity was engulfed in pus flakes.

thoracic working ports were inserted and finally with a combination of traction from above and below the herniated stomach was reduced into the abdomen and on inspecting it a large perforation was found along the greater curvature near the fundus.
The anaesthetist was then asked to insert a nasogastric tube which was quickly passed along the perforation into the distal stomach.
The final remaining adhesions were then released.
Given the extensive size of the perforation, we decided to perform a stapled resection of the devitalized portion of the stomach.
A linear 60 mm endo-stapling device was introduced and fired three times across the body and the fundus of the stomach to resect out the perforation.
The presence of the thoracic optical port provided a unique view of this resection from above.
A final clip was applied and the perforated portion of the stomach was cut away.
Following this, generous lavage and aspiration was performed in the thoracic cavity using normal saline so as to minimize the local contamination and prevent sepsis in the post-operative period.
We then proceeded to repair the diaphragmatic rent.
The thoracic side provided better visualization and more convenience in suturing and hence the repair was performed from that side.
Since the thoracic working ports were only of 5 mm size, the suture was introduced through an abdominal working port and passed into the thorax intracorporeally through the diaphragmatic defect.
The margins of the defect were freshened and the repair was performed using a 2-0 barbed polygalactone suture material with an absorption profile of 180 days.
The bites were taken in a continuous manner on either side, ensuring adequate distance from the margins of the defect.
The repair was done primarily without the use of a mesh given the extensive contamination present in the surgical field.
The needle was finally passed through the abdominal side and a last bite was taken following which the suture was secured with the help of a clip.
Then a gauze was introduced into the thoracic cavity and further mopping was done.
As the procedure was performed with the help of single lung ventilation, the anaesthetist was asked to inflate the lung which expanded well.
An intercostal chest tube was placed through one of the thoracic working ports to facilitate drainage of any residual effusion in the pleural cavity.
Final lavage and mopping of the abdomen was done and lastly specimen was retrieved.
An abdominal drain was placed and the port sites were closed back.
